Member-only story

Why the best candidates aren’t the ones who memorize the most JavaScript — they’re the ones who solve the hardest engineering problems.

5 min read
1 hour ago

--

Walk into almost any JavaScript interview preparation course and you’ll see the same pattern.
Hundreds of questions about closures.
Dozens of exercises on promises.
A long list of array methods to memorize.
A reminder to review this, prototypes, and the event loop.
None of these topics are unimportant. But there is a subtle problem.
They are preparing you for JavaScript quizzes, not JavaScript jobs.
The longer I work with production software, the more obvious this becomes. The developers who consistently receive offers are rarely the ones who know the most syntax. They’re the ones who demonstrate something much harder to teach: engineering judgment.
That distinction is becoming even more important as AI writes increasing amounts of code.
Companies are no longer asking, “Can this person write JavaScript?”
